articles.title: What was known? Holocaust-era letters as sources of knowledge production
articles.abstract: Abstract This article explores how private letters, which were preserved by Jewish refugees who migrated before and after the Holocaust to Britain, and which were eventually donated to the Wiener Holocaust Library (WHL) in London, reveal evidence of how “Holocaust knowledge” was produced, assimilated, spread, and transformed. The authors argue that analyzing these letters as sites of knowledge production reinforces the role of individuals in forming and exchanging knowledge and articulates the multiple mediating interventions of the archive. As an institution born of persecution and forced Jewish migration during the Nazi period, the WHL is a particularly useful archive from which to draw private correspondence for this study and to analyze the changing roles and interventions of archives on migrant knowledge. This article traces rumor, “false” information, and subversive forms of knowledge exchange in the correspondence; as well as the “afterlives” of the material letters held in the archives, and the strategies (sources of information, existing forms of knowledge, culturally embedded narratives) that victims and survivors used to make sense of what was happening to them as events unfolded and were described, and as knowledge was shared and transformed through space and time.

articles.title: “I have very regrettable news to send you today”: Australia and global information networks during the Holocaust
articles.abstract: Abstract This article analyzes information networks between Australia and Nazi-controlled Europe during the Holocaust. Around ten thousand Jewish refugees, migrants, and detainees survived the Holocaust in Australia. Although they had been saved, almost all of them left relatives behind in Europe, including, in some cases, their spouses, parents, and children. Scholars have traditionally assumed that the outbreak of the war cut all connections and that refugees, with minor exceptions, learned about the fate of their family members only after the war. This article challenges this assumption by focusing on wartime avenues, networks, and connections that facilitated the transmission of personal information between Nazi-controlled Europe and Australia. It argues that despite imposed restrictions on communication, Jews in Australia managed to receive up-to-date information about their family members, often including their deportation and location in Eastern Europe.

articles.title: Mom Matters: Shaping Religious Identity in Kids Born to Jewish Intermarriages
articles.abstract: Abstract Recent studies on intermarriage within the Jewish population examine the community’s integration into broader society and the factors that shape it. This paper examines changes in intermarriage and fertility within the Jewish population in Brazil, based on data from the 1980–2010 censuses. We show that the proportion of intermarriages has been increasing in Brazil following the same trend observed in the USA. The paper also discusses the religious and ethnic identity of children born to Jewish intermarriages. The empirical analysis shows that the likelihood of children from intermarriages identifying as Jewish is higher when they have a Jewish mother and live in areas with a well-established Jewish community. Furthermore, the impact of having a Jewish mother on the religious identity of children born to mixed Jewish marriages has increased significantly over time. The results support both theoretical hypotheses regarding a Brazilian-style integration of the Jewish community into society and broader changes in family patterns affecting the entire society.
